_purpose: County Landscape Profile for Greene County, AL. Physical geography, sensory snapshots, and landscape change narratives. Greene County — seat at Eutaw — represents the extreme of Black Belt plantation economy: massive enslaved population, immense wealth concentrated in few hands, and total economic devastation after the Civil War that persisted for over a century.

physical_geography.physiographic_province: Black Belt Prairie section of the East Gulf Coastal Plain. Classic Black Belt topography — rolling prairie on Selma Chalk with deeply incised creek valleys. The Black Warrior River forms the northern boundary.
physical_geography.elevation_range: 100–400 ft. The Black Warrior River bottoms are the lowest terrain; the prairie uplands rise gently to 350–400 ft in the southern portion.
physical_geography.dominant_terrain: Rolling chalk prairie — the archetypal Black Belt landscape. Dark, heavy clay uplands dissected by creeks draining to the Black Warrior and Tombigbee rivers. The terrain is gently rolling, not flat, with broad swells of prairie separated by wooded creek valleys. The contrast between the open prairie and the forested creek bottoms is the defining visual feature.

soil_types.description: Dark, heavy calcareous clay — the Black Belt's signature soil. Formed from Selma Chalk weathering. Cracks deeply in summer; gumbo when wet.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Produced extraordinary cotton yields that made Greene County's planters among the wealthiest in Alabama. The labor required to work this soil was immense and was extracted from enslaved people under conditions of relentless physical demand.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Greene County, Alabama. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1917.

soil_types.description: Heavy montmorillonite clay in the transition zones. Swelling clay that buckled roads and foundations.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Difficult to manage. The swelling properties made this soil treacherous for structures and roads. Farms on Vaiden clay were less productive than those on Houston clay.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Greene County, Alabama. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1917.

major_waterways.name: Black Warrior River
major_waterways.role: Northern county boundary, transportation to Tuscaloosa and beyond
major_waterways.description: The Black Warrior flows along Greene County's northern border, connecting the county to Tuscaloosa upstream and the Tombigbee system downstream. River landings along the northern edge of the county served plantation commerce.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Black Warrior River carried Greene County's cotton toward Tuscaloosa and Mobile — {ancestor_name} lived on the prairie south of the river, where the dark soil made fortunes that flowed downstream with every bale.

major_waterways.name: Sipsey River
major_waterways.description: A tributary of the Tombigbee draining the western portion of Greene County. The Sipsey valley was plantation country, with some of the largest slaveholdings in the county along its banks.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Sipsey valley plantations were among Greene County's largest — {ancestor_name} knew the river as the boundary between one man's land and another's, with enslaved families on both sides whose labor made the boundary meaningful.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Tallgrass prairie with scattered post oak and cedar; dense hardwood forest along creek valleys and the Black Warrior bottoms
vegetation_eras.description: Open grassland on the chalk uplands, maintained by Creek and Choctaw burning. The creek valleys carried dense forest — oak, hickory, sweetgum, and cypress in the wetter bottoms. The prairie was not barren but richly vegetated with native grasses that had never been plowed.
vegetation_eras.source: Hilliard, Atlas of Antebellum Southern Agriculture (1984).

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Cotton monoculture on the cleared prairie; plantation complexes as landscape features; forest only in creek ravines
vegetation_eras.description: The conversion was total. By 1860, Greene County had one of the highest enslaved population densities in the South. The landscape was defined by cotton from horizon to horizon on the uplands, with the plantation headquarters — great house, slave quarters, gin house, smokehouse — visible as clustered nodes on the rolling terrain.
vegetation_eras.source: Rogers et al., Alabama: The History of a Deep South State (1994).

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Fragmented cotton fields; cedar-broomsedge succession on abandoned land; sharecropper cabins replacing slave quarters
vegetation_eras.description: The postbellum landscape was one of progressive decline. Cotton fields shrank. Abandoned land reverted to cedar scrub. The great houses deteriorated without the wealth to maintain them. Sharecropper cabins — small, unpainted, chimney at one end — became the characteristic structure. By 1960, Greene County was among the poorest and most depopulated counties in Alabama.
vegetation_eras.source: Daniel, Breaking the Land (1985). Flynt, Poor but Proud (1989).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: In November on a Greene County plantation, the cotton picking was winding down and the gin house was running day and night. The sound of the gin — the grinding of the rollers separating seed from fiber — carried across the open prairie like mechanical breathing. Wagon loads of seed cotton waited in the yard, and the lint room filled with compressed bales bound in jute bagging. The air smelled of cotton lint, mule dung, and the sharp smell of new-cut pine from the press beams. Enslaved workers moved between the field and the gin in a rhythm dictated by the overseer's schedule — pick until dark, gin by lantern light. The prairie stretched away from the gin house in every direction, the cotton plants stripped and brown, the dark soil showing between the rows. The temperature had dropped enough to make the mornings sharp, and enslaved workers without adequate clothing shivered through the pre-dawn assembling that preceded each day's labor.
sensory_snapshots.source: Hilliard, Atlas of Antebellum Southern Agriculture (1984). Kolchin, First Freedom (1972).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: A March morning in Greene County at the turn of the century was the quietest time in the quietest place in Alabama. The sharecroppers were beginning to break ground for spring planting, but the fields were smaller now — fewer families, fewer mules, fewer acres under cultivation. The prairie had a different look than in the antebellum days: cedar trees had colonized the abandoned fields, and the old slave quarters had collapsed or been scavenged for lumber. The air smelled of wet clay and new growth — dogwood blooming white against the dark creek-bottom forest, redbud adding purple to the understory. The crossroads store at the nearest intersection was the only commercial establishment for miles, its porch the gathering point for a community that had shrunk with every decade. The quiet was not peace — it was the silence of a place that had been emptied of its people.

On a summer night in Greene County during Reconstruction, the darkness on the open prairie was absolute except for the light from cabin windows and the occasional torch of a patrol. The air was heavy with heat and humidity, thick with the sound of insects and the distant barking of dogs. In the Black community — the overwhelming majority of the county — families gathered in church buildings and cabin clearings to organize politically under the new constitutional dispensation. The risk was real: white-supremacist violence was escalating across the Black Belt, and Greene County's Black political leaders faced threats, beatings, and assassination. The darkness that sheltered these meetings also sheltered the Klan riders who moved between plantations on roads they had always owned.

landscape_changes.change: Creek and Choctaw cession to cotton kingdom
landscape_changes.description: Greene County was created in 1819 from territory ceded by the Creek and Choctaw nations. American planters from Georgia, Virginia, and the Carolinas rushed in with enslaved labor forces. Within twenty years, the prairie was transformed from native grassland to one of the most productive cotton zones in the South.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: When {ancestor_name} arrived in Greene County, {pronoun_subject} came to land taken from the Creek and Choctaw within living memory — the native place names survived, but the people who had given them were gone.
landscape_changes.source: Rogers et al., Alabama: The History of a Deep South State (1994).

landscape_changes.description: Greene County lost population in every census from 1900 to 1960. The boll weevil, soil exhaustion, and the pull of northern industrial jobs drained the Black majority. White population also declined. The county's tax base collapsed, public services deteriorated, and the landscape returned to something closer to its pre-settlement state — cedar and grass reclaiming what cotton and labor had once held.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: By {year}, Greene County was emptying — {ancestor_name}'s neighbors leaving for Birmingham, Chicago, Detroit — and the dark prairie was growing up in cedar and broomsedge, the land reclaiming what the cotton economy could no longer hold.

narrative_hooks: Greene County's enslaved population in 1860 was among the most concentrated in the South — {ancestor_name} lived in a county where the dark soil and the forced labor that worked it produced wealth visible in the great houses and invisible in the human cost.

The Eutaw courthouse stood on the prairie like a monument to the planter class — when {ancestor_name} conducted business there in {year}, the building represented a legal system built to protect property, including the property of one human being in another.

By {year}, the cedar trees advancing across Greene County's abandoned cotton fields told the story that the census confirmed — the people were leaving, and the land was returning to something the prairie grasses recognized.

The crossroads store in Greene County was the last point of economic contact — {ancestor_name} settled the year's accounts there in {year}, the merchant's ledger the final word on whether the family's labor had produced enough to survive.

The Black Warrior River at Greene County's northern edge carried the cotton to market and the county's name into the record — but the river did not carry the stories of the people who picked it, which traveled north by rail and memory.
source_references: Rogers, William Warren, Robert David Ward, Leah Rawls Atkins, and Wayne Flynt. Alabama: The History of a Deep South State. Tuscaloosa: University of Alabama Press, 1994.
Kolchin, Peter. First Freedom: The Responses of Alabama's Blacks to Emancipation and Reconstruction. Westport, CT: Greenwood Press, 1972.
Daniel, Pete. Breaking the Land: The Transformation of Cotton, Tobacco, and Rice Cultures since 1880. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 1985.
Hilliard, Sam Bowers. Atlas of Antebellum Southern Agriculture. Baton Rouge: Louisiana State University Press, 1984.
Flynt, Wayne. Poor but Proud: Alabama's Poor Whites. Tuscaloosa: University of Alabama Press, 1989.
Foner, Eric. Reconstruction: America's Unfinished Revolution, 1863–1877. New York: Harper & Row, 1988.
USDA Soil Survey of Greene County, Alabama. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1917.
